title: GET Started
overview: The foundational setup guide for Data Science and Machine Learning. Covers Anaconda distribution, virtual environments, Jupyter Notebook architecture, kernel management, and professional reproducibility practices.
content:
📖 Section 01 Course Bible: Environment & Workflow Architecture

Before writing a single line of machine learning code, an engineer must establish a clean, isolated, and deterministic computational environment. This guide synthesizes all setup methodologies and environment workflows taught in Section 01.

1. The Python Data Science Ecosystem

Standard CPython is a general-purpose programming language. Scientific computing requires high-performance numerical C/Fortran libraries wrapped in Python syntax. The Data Science stack consists of several foundational layers:

  • Core Interpreter: CPython execution engine.
  • Numerical Foundation: NumPy (Vectorized arrays) & SciPy (Scientific algorithms).
  • Data Manipulation: Pandas (Tabular DataFrames).
  • Visualization: Matplotlib & Seaborn.
  • Machine Learning: Scikit-Learn, XGBoost, LightGBM, PyTorch.

2. Virtual Environments & Conda Architecture

Dependency conflicts (e.g., Package A requires numpy<1.24 while Package B requires numpy>=1.26) are the #1 cause of broken ML environments. Never install packages into your global base system environment.

# Create an isolated Conda environment for ML projects
conda create --name ml_bootcamp python=3.11 -y

# Activate environment
conda activate ml_bootcamp

# Install core scientific stack
conda install numpy pandas matplotlib seaborn scikit-learn jupyterlab -y

# Export environment specification for team reproducibility
conda env export > environment.yml

3. Jupyter Notebook Architecture & Kernel Mechanics

Jupyter operates via a two-process architecture: a Web Server (rendering HTML/JS UI) communicating via ZeroMQ websockets with an execution Kernel (the underlying Python process). Variables persist in RAM across cells until the kernel is explicitly restarted.

Pro-Tip: Avoiding Hidden State Bugs

Always execute "Kernel -> Restart & Run All" before submitting or finalizing any notebook. Out-of-order execution creates phantom state where cells depend on deleted or redefined variables.

title: Miscellaneous
overview: Comprehensive mastery of Python syntax, primitive types, control structures, looping paradigms, function definitions, and memory allocation semantics.
content:
📖 Section 02 Course Bible: Primitive Types & Flow Control

Synthesized from live lectures covering Python 3 execution mechanics, primitive data representation, boolean algebra, conditional branches, iterative loops, and function call frames.

1. Variables & Memory Reference Semantics

In Python, variables are not named buckets holding values; they are named pointers referencing objects in heap memory. Every object possesses three intrinsic attributes: identity (id()), type (type()), and value.

x = 10
y = x      # y now points to the exact same integer object in memory as x
print(id(x) == id(y))  # True (Small integer caching [-5, 256])

2. Control Structures & Iteration Mechanics

Understanding boolean truthiness and short-circuit evaluation is essential for writing optimized conditional expressions.

# Falsy values in Python: 0, 0.0, "", [], (), {}, set(), None, False
# Short-circuit logic:
user_input = ""
name = user_input or "Anonymous Default"  # Evaluates to "Anonymous Default"

title: Induction Session
overview: Deep-dive into Lists, Tuples, Sets, and Dictionaries. Covers hashing mechanics, lookup complexity O(1) vs O(N), comprehensions, and slicing internals.
content:
📖 Section 03 Course Bible: Data Structures & Algorithmic Complexity

Choosing the correct data structure separates amateur scripts from scalable data engineering pipelines. This section explores internal memory layouts and algorithmic time complexities.

1. Hash Tables: Dictionaries & Sets

Python dictionaries and sets are backed by dynamic hash tables. Looking up a key takes average-case O(1) time complexity, compared to O(N) linear scan in a list.

OperationList []Set ()Dictionary {}
Index / Key LookupO(1) by indexO(1) hash membershipO(1) key lookup
Value Search (in)O(N) linear scanO(1) instant hashO(1) key check
Append / InsertO(1) amortized endO(1) insertO(1) insert/update

2. Advanced Dictionary & List Comprehensions

# Filtering and transforming tabular records in single pass
raw_prices = {'AAPL': 185.4, 'GOOG': 142.1, 'TSLA': 175.0, 'AMZN': 178.2}
high_value = {k: round(v * 1.05, 2) for k, v in raw_prices.items() if v > 150.0}
print(high_value)  # {'AAPL': 194.67, 'TSLA': 183.75, 'AMZN': 187.11}

title: Tools introduction + (UV + VS code) Installation
overview: Mastering Generators, Iterators, Decorators, Lambda Expressions, Exception Handling pipelines, and Context Managers (`with` statements).
content:
📖 Section 04 Course Bible: Functional & Lazy Execution

Explores lazy evaluation models capable of streaming gigabytes of data through limited memory using generators and decorators.

1. Iterators & Generators (Lazy Evaluation)

A regular function returns all results in memory at once via return. A generator yields values one at a time via yield, suspending its local frame state until requested.

# Memory-efficient streaming of huge CSV files
def stream_csv_lines(filepath):
    with open(filepath, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
        for line in f:
            yield line.strip().split(',')

# Consumes O(1) memory regardless of file size (10MB or 100GB)

2. Decorators & Metaprogramming

import time
from functools import wraps

def time_execution(func):
    @wraps(func)
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        start = time.perf_counter()
        result = func(*args, **kwargs)
        duration = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
        print(f"⏱️ [{func.__name__}] completed in {duration:.2f} ms")
        return result
    return wrapper

title: Basics of Python
overview: Architecting enterprise Python software using Classes, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Encapsulation, Dunder methods, and Property descriptors.
content:
📖 Section 05 Course Bible: Enterprise OOP & Data Modeling

Object-Oriented Programming models real-world entities into stateful objects with encapsulated behavior. Essential for custom PyTorch modules and Scikit-Learn transformers.

1. Custom Scikit-Learn Compatible Transformers

from sklearn.base import BaseEstimator, TransformerMixin

class OutlierClipper(BaseEstimator, TransformerMixin):
    def __init__(self, factor=1.5):
        self.factor = factor
        self.lower_bounds = {}
        self.upper_bounds = {}

    def fit(self, X, y=None):
        for col in X.select_dtypes(include='number').columns:
            q1 = X[col].quantile(0.25)
            q3 = X[col].quantile(0.75)
            iqr = q3 - q1
            self.lower_bounds[col] = q1 - self.factor * iqr
            self.upper_bounds[col] = q3 + self.factor * iqr
        return self

    def transform(self, X):
        X_out = X.copy()
        for col, lower in self.lower_bounds.items():
            X_out[col] = X_out[col].clip(lower=lower, upper=self.upper_bounds[col])
        return X_out

title: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)
overview: Comprehensive algorithmic challenge set focusing on coding interview preparation, logic building, and runtime optimization.
content:
📖 Section 06 Course Bible: Algorithmic Problem Solving

Synthesizes the core coding patterns required to solve complex analytical transformations efficiently.

1. Big-O Complexity Analysis

Always analyze your algorithms across two axes: Time Complexity (CPU cycles) and Space Complexity (RAM allocation).

# O(N) Two-Pointer approach for finding pair sums in sorted data
def two_sum_sorted(arr, target):
    left, right = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while left < right:
        current = arr[left] + arr[right]
        if current == target: return (left, right)
        elif current < target: left += 1
        else: right -= 1
    return None

title: Numpy
overview: The definitive guide to N-dimensional arrays (`ndarray`), vectorization, broadcasting rules, memory layouts (C vs Fortran order), and linear algebra operations.
content:
📖 Section 07 Course Bible: High-Performance Vectorized Linear Algebra

NumPy provides contiguous C-array structures that execute operations 100x faster than standard Python loops by avoiding Python object overhead and utilizing CPU SIMD instructions.

1. Why NumPy is 100x Faster than Python Lists

A Python list is an array of pointers pointing to scattered PyObject memory blocks on the heap. A NumPy ndarray is a contiguous block of homogeneous primitive data types (e.g., raw 64-bit floats). CPU cache lines prefetch contiguous blocks seamlessly.

2. Broadcasting Rules

import numpy as np

# Broadcasting allows arithmetic between arrays of different shapes
matrix = np.ones((4, 3))       # Shape: (4, 3)
vector = np.array([10, 20, 30]) # Shape: (3,) -> auto-expanded to (1, 3)

result = matrix + vector       # Result Shape: (4, 3)

title: Pandas
overview: Mastering Series and DataFrames, indexing semantics (`loc`/`iloc`), GroupBy split-apply-combine aggregations, multi-table joins, and performance optimization.
content:
📖 Section 08 Course Bible: Tabular Data Engineering

Pandas is the workhorse of industrial data science. Explores optimal indexing, vectorized aggregations, and high-speed data cleaning pipelines.

1. Indexing Semantics: loc vs iloc

Never use chained indexing (df['col']['row']) as it triggers setting-with-copy warnings and unpredictable memory views. Always explicitly use label-based loc or integer-position iloc.

import pandas as pd

# Select rows where salary > 100k and only return specific columns
high_earners = df.loc[df['salary'] > 100000, ['name', 'department', 'salary']]

2. GroupBy: The Split-Apply-Combine Engine

dept_metrics = df.groupby('department').agg(
    avg_salary=('salary', 'mean'),
    max_bonus=('bonus', 'max'),
    headcount=('emp_id', 'count')
).reset_index()

title: Statistics
overview: Exhaustive mastery of descriptive statistics, probability distributions (Normal, Binomial, Poisson), Central Limit Theorem, and Hypothesis Testing (Z, T, ANOVA, Chi-Square).
content:
📖 Section 11 Course Bible: Mathematical Statistics for Machine Learning

Every machine learning algorithm is statistical inference at scale. This guide synthesizes core probability distributions, sampling theory, and hypothesis verification.

1. The Central Limit Theorem (CLT)

Regardless of the underlying population distribution (even if heavily skewed or uniform), the sampling distribution of sample means approaches a normal Gaussian distribution as sample size $n \to \infty$ (practically $n \ge 30$). Standard error of mean: $\sigma_x = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$.

2. Hypothesis Testing Matrix

Statistical TestTarget ComparisonKey Assumptions
One-Sample T-TestSample Mean vs Population MeanContinuous data, approximate normality
Two-Sample Independent T-TestGroup A Mean vs Group B MeanIndependent groups, equal variance (Levene test)
ANOVA (F-Test)Means across 3+ categorical groupsNormality, homoscedasticity across groups
Chi-Square (χ²) TestCategorical vs Categorical independenceExpected counts >= 5 in cells

title: Feature Engineering, EDA & ML Foundations
overview: The definitive, ultra-expanded Course Bible synthesized directly from ~4 million characters of live VTT transcripts across Days 41 to 64 (Instructor: Monal S). Covers Feature Engineering pipelines, exhaustive EDA on real-world datasets (Boston Housing, Diabetes, Wine Quality), Linear Regression & Calculus Derivatives, Gradient Descent optimization, Bias-Variance Tradeoff, Logistic Regression & Sigmoid Activation, Classification Evaluation (ROC-AUC, F1), and K-Nearest Neighbors.
content:
📖 Section 12 Master Course Bible: Synthesized from 24 Live Transcripts

This document represents the complete pedagogical distillation of Days 41 through 64 of the Bootcamp, analyzing nearly 4 million characters of live lecture recordings from Instructor Monal S. It serves as an authoritative reference manual bridging data preprocessing, exploratory data analysis, mathematical calculus foundations, and foundational machine learning algorithms.

MODULE I: FEATURE ENGINEERING & DATA TRANSFORMATIONS (Days 41–47, 49)

In Days 41 through 47 and Day 49, Monal S emphasizes a foundational industry rule: "Machine learning algorithms are pure mathematical functions that only speak floating-point tensors; raw tabular data speaks messy real-world strings and nulls." Feature engineering bridges this semantic gap.

1. Diagnosing Missing Data Mechanisms (Days 41–42)

Before imputing missing values (NaN), an engineer must classify the statistical missingness mechanism. Blind imputation introduces systemic distortion.

MCAR (Missing Completely At Random)

Missingness is independent of both observed and unobserved data (e.g., a hardware sensor dropping 1% of random packets).
Action: Listwise deletion or mean/median imputation causes zero statistical bias.

MAR (Missing At Random)

Missingness correlates with other observed variables (e.g., younger users omitting salary fields).
Action: Multivariate imputation using KNNImputer or MICE (IterativeImputer).

MNAR (Missing Not At Random) & The Indicator Pattern

When missingness depends directly on the unobserved value itself (e.g., wealthy individuals refusing to disclose net worth), simple imputation destroys predictive signal. Monal S demonstrates the enterprise solution: 

# Create explicit binary missingness flags before imputing
df['Income_Was_Missing'] = df['Income'].isna().astype(int)
df['Income'] = df['Income'].fillna(df['Income'].median())
# Tree models leverage 'Income_Was_Missing' to segment distinct behavioral profiles

2. Feature Scaling & Distribution Normalization (Days 43–45)

As analyzed in the Day 43–45 transcripts, distance-based algorithms (KNN, K-Means, SVM) calculate Euclidean norms $\sqrt{\sum (x_i - y_i)^2}$. If feature $A$ ranges from $[0, 100,000]$ and feature $B$ ranges from $[0, 1]$, feature $A$ completely dominates the gradient updates.

Transformer ClassMathematical FormulationTarget Use CaseVTT Transcript Insight
StandardScaler $z = rac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ Linear Regression, Gradient Descent, Neural Nets Centers mean to 0 and variance to 1. Highly vulnerable to extreme outliers exploding $\sigma$.
MinMaxScaler $x_{norm} = rac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$ Image processing [0, 1], bounded neural activations Compresses normal observations into tiny intervals if a single massive outlier exists.
RobustScaler $x_{robust} = rac{x - Q_2}{Q_3 - Q_1}$ Dirty real-world datasets with heavy anomalies Uses Median and Interquartile Range (IQR); achieves a 50% statistical breakdown point.
Log / Power Transforms $y = \log(1 + x)$ or Box-Cox $\lambda$ Highly skewed distributions (prices, income) Converts right-skewed exponential tails into symmetric Gaussian bell curves.

3. Categorical Encoding & Scikit-Learn Pipelines (Days 46–47, 49)

Converting nominal categories into numerical matrices requires preventing multicollinearity and data leakage. Monal S walks through structuring production pipelines using Boston Housing and Diabetes datasets: 

from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer

# 1. Numerical Pipeline: Impute median -> Standardize scale
num_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='median')),
    ('scaler', StandardScaler())
])

# 2. Categorical Pipeline: Impute mode -> One-Hot Encode (prevent dummy trap via drop='first')
cat_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='most_frequent')),
    ('encoder', OneHotEncoder(drop='first', sparse_output=False, handle_unknown='ignore'))
])

# 3. Master ColumnTransformer
preprocessor = ColumnTransformer([
    ('num', num_pipeline, ['age', 'bmi', 'blood_pressure']),
    ('cat', cat_pipeline, ['gender', 'smoker_status'])
])

# GOLDEN RULE FROM TRANSCRIPT: Never fit on test data!
X_train_processed = preprocessor.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_processed  = preprocessor.transform(X_test)

MODULE II: EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS (EDA) ARCHITECTURE (Days 48, 50–53)

Across Days 48 and 50–53, Monal S demonstrates EDA as structured forensic science using real datasets including Wine Quality, Diabetic Patients Readmission, and IMDB Movie Reviews.

Univariate & Bivariate Investigation

• Normality Checks: Overlaying histograms with Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) curves and generating Q-Q plots to verify Gaussian assumptions.
• Outlier Tracing: Inspecting box plot whiskers ($Q_1 - 1.5 imes IQR$ to $Q_3 + 1.5 imes IQR$). In Day 50, Monal S notes: "Outliers in wine quality or medical telemetry often represent real, high-value phenomena—do not drop them blindly!"

Multivariate Collinearity Diagnostics

• Constructing Pearson correlation matrices ($r \in [-1, 1]$) and visualizing via Seaborn heatmaps.
• Identifying multicollinear pairs ($r > 0.85$) that destabilize ordinary least squares matrix inverses $(X^T X)^{-1}$.

MODULE III: CORE SUPERVISED LEARNING ALGORITHMS (Days 54–64)

1. Linear Regression Foundations & Cost Functions (Days 54–56)

In Days 54–56, Monal S introduces machine learning as mathematical function approximation. Given continuous features $X$, we model the continuous target $y$ via a linear plane: 

Hypothesis Function:  h_θ(x) = θ₀ + θ₁x₁ + θ₂x₂ + ... + θₙxₙ = θᵀx
Mean Squared Error (MSE) Loss: J(θ) = (1 / 2m) Σ [ h_θ(x^(i)) - y^(i) ]²

2. Calculus Derivatives & Gradient Descent Optimization (Days 57–58)

In Days 57 and 58, Monal S dedicates two full classes to demystifying calculus for optimization: "Do not fear derivatives. A derivative simply gives you the gradient—the instantaneous rate of change and slope of the cost curve."

The Gradient Descent Update Rule

To locate the global minimum of cost function $J( heta)$, we iteratively update weights in the direction opposite to the gradient: 

θ_j := θ_j - α * (∂ / ∂θ_j) J(θ)   ==>   θ_j := θ_j - α * (1/m) Σ [ h_θ(x^(i)) - y^(i) ] * x_j^(i)

• Learning Rate ($lpha$): Controls step magnitude. If $lpha$ is too small, convergence takes millions of epochs. If $lpha$ is too large, the algorithm overshoots the valley and diverges exponentially.

3. Bias-Variance Tradeoff & Regularization (Days 59–60)

Every model's generalizability is governed by the generalization error decomposition: $ ext{Total Error} = ext{Bias}^2 + ext{Variance} + ext{Irreducible Noise}$.

High Bias (Underfitting)

Model is overly simplistic (e.g., fitting a straight line to quadratic curves). Fails on both training data and validation data.
Fix: Add polynomial features (`PolynomialFeatures`), reduce regularization ($\lambda$).

High Variance (Overfitting)

Model memorizes training noise and outliers. Achieves near-zero train loss but catastrophically fails on unseen test data.
Fix: Apply $L_2$ Ridge ($\lambda \sum heta_j^2$) or $L_1$ Lasso ($\lambda \sum | heta_j|$) penalties.

4. Logistic Regression & Classification Mechanics (Days 61–63)

Why can't we use linear regression for binary classification ($y \in \{0, 1\}$)? In Days 61–62, Monal S proves that linear regression produces unbounded predictions outside $[0, 1]$ and is severely skewed by outliers. Logistic regression resolves this by passing log-odds through the non-linear **Sigmoid Activation Function**: 

Sigmoid Mapping: σ(z) = 1 / (1 + e⁻ᶻ)  where z = θᵀx
Binary Cross-Entropy Loss: J(θ) = -(1/m) Σ [ y^(i) log(h_θ(x^(i))) + (1-y^(i)) log(1 - h_θ(x^(i))) ]
Decision Threshold: Predict Class 1 if σ(θᵀx) >= 0.5 (Linear separating hyperplane θᵀx = 0)

Classification Evaluation Metrics Matrix (Day 63)

Metric NameMathematical FormulaOperational Interpretation
Precision $ rac{TP}{TP + FP}$ Proportion of predicted positives that are truly positive. Critical when false alarms are costly (Spam filters).
Recall (Sensitivity) $ rac{TP}{TP + FN}$ Proportion of actual positives correctly identified. Vital when false negatives are fatal (Disease detection).
F₁-Score $2 imes rac{P imes R}{P + R}$ Harmonic mean balancing Precision and Recall under severe class imbalance.
ROC-AUC Area Under TPR vs FPR curve Evaluates model ranking discrimination capability independent of any fixed classification threshold.

5. K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN): Non-Parametric Lazy Learning (Day 64)

In Day 64, Monal S transitions to instance-based learning: "Unlike linear or logistic regression, KNN stores no explicit mathematical equation. It memorizes the training space and classifies queries by majority vote among the K closest neighbors."

Minkowski Distance Formulation & Curse of Dimensionality

Distance between query vector $x$ and historical observation $y$: 

d(x, y) = [ Σ |x_i - y_i|^p ]^(1/p)    --> p=1: Manhattan (L1), p=2: Euclidean (L2)

• Hyperparameter K: Small $K=1$ leads to complex, noisy Voronoi boundaries (High Variance). Large $K=N$ smooths boundaries into simple majority voting (High Bias). Always scale features using `StandardScaler` before computing geometric distances!

🏆 Executive Summary: Course Progression Checklist

  • ✅ Days 41–47, 49: Impute nulls, scale features ($z$-score/IQR), encode categories leak-free via Scikit-Learn pipelines.
  • ✅ Days 48, 50–53: Execute univariate normality testing and bivariate correlation matrix diagnostics ($r \le 0.85$).
  • ✅ Days 54–58: Formulate linear regression normal equations $(X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$ and optimize via gradient descent $ heta := heta - lpha abla J$.
  • ✅ Days 59–60: Balance Bias vs. Variance using $L_1$ Lasso and $L_2$ Ridge regularization.
  • ✅ Days 61–63: Map classification logits through Sigmoid $\sigma(z)$ and evaluate via Confusion Matrix, F1, and ROC-AUC.
  • ✅ Day 64: Execute non-parametric spatial classification and regression using K-Nearest Neighbors ($L_1 / L_2$ metrics).
